task: I’m putting together a short post-production checklist for a freelance editor before picture lock, and I want it to catch the stuff that usually slips through. Can you help me draft one that’s practical and not overkill?

prefs.explanation: He seems to value getting to the point without extra noise, especially given how he works under deadline pressure. A response that is too long or meandering would likely feel inefficient to him. He probably still wants enough detail to act, but not a lot of filler.

His communication style in work is straightforward: explain the issue, name the delay, and state the next step. He likely prefers the same from an assistant rather than softened hints or vague hedging. Directness fits his honest, exacting temperament.

He seems to respond well to order and clean workflow, so scattered or exploratory replies would probably frustrate him. Structured guidance would match how he already thinks about edits, revisions, and process templates. He likely appreciates when the assistant lays things out in a predictable sequence.

prefs.short: Wants the main points plus key caveats
prefs.explanation: He cares about technical correctness, so bare headlines are probably not enough when a mistake could matter. At the same time, he likely does not want exhaustive theory unless it directly helps him act. A focused answer with the important caveats is the best fit.

prefs.short: Responds best to direct, constructive critique
prefs.explanation: He appears to prefer honest correction over vague reassurance, especially when quality or timing is at stake. Pointing out what is wrong and how to fix it would likely be more useful to him than overly gentle framing. He probably respects critique that is specific and practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical, pre-picture-lock post-production checklist for a freelance editor that catches the usual misses without turning into a monster document.

## Pre–Picture Lock Checklist
**Editor:**
**Project:**
**Date:**

### 1) Timeline / Cut Integrity
- [ ] Confirm latest approved sequence is open
- [ ] Check for any accidental duplicate or offline clips
- [ ] Verify no temp shots or placeholders remain unless approved
- [ ] Confirm all transitions are intentional and clean
- [ ] Check for stray frames, flash frames, or black frames
- [ ] Review all end cuts for clean handles and no missing frames
- [ ] Confirm all speed ramps / retimes are correct and intentional

### 2) Audio
- [ ] Check all dialogue is present and synced
- [ ] Review audio levels for obvious jumps or clipping
- [ ] Confirm temp music/SFX are correct and not missing
- [ ] Remove any scratch tracks that should not carry through
- [ ] Check for pops, clicks, hum, or accidental mute regions
- [ ] Verify J/L cuts are intentional and clean
- [ ] Confirm no unintended audio gaps at edit points

### 3) Graphics / Text
- [ ] Spellcheck all on-screen text, supers, and lower thirds
- [ ] Confirm names, titles, dates, and URLs are correct
- [ ] Check safe margins / title safe placement
- [ ] Verify end cards, logos, and legal lines are correct
- [ ] Confirm any placeholder graphics are replaced
- [ ] Check font consistency and styling across the cut

### 4) Color / Visual Consistency
- [ ] Check for shots that are obviously mismatched in exposure or color
- [ ] Confirm no uncorrected test grades or temp LUT issues remain
- [ ] Look for flicker, banding, or weird frame-to-frame changes
- [ ] Verify any VFX or composited shots are approved and clean
- [ ] Check for missing masks, mattes, or visible edges

### 5) Media / Conform Readiness
- [ ] Confirm all source media is linked correctly
- [ ] Check for offline media or relink warnings
- [ ] Verify all used clips are in the project/bin
- [ ] Confirm version names and sequence naming are clear
- [ ] Make sure exports will pull from the correct sequence

### 6) Final Watchthrough
- [ ] Watch the full cut from start to finish without stopping
- [ ] Note any last-minute issues or questionable moments
- [ ] Check first and last 10 seconds carefully
- [ ] Confirm overall pacing and continuity still hold
- [ ] Verify the cut matches the latest notes / client direction

### 7) Deliverables Prep
- [ ] Confirm export settings match delivery specs
- [ ] Check that the filename is correct
- [ ] Verify burn-ins, captions, or timecode if required
- [ ] Export a review file and spot-check playback
- [ ] Save a clean archive of the project/version

---

## Shorter “Must-Catch” Version
If you want something leaner, these are the big ones that usually slip:
- [ ] No temp shots, placeholders, or offline clips
- [ ] No stray frames, black frames, or flash frames
- [ ] Audio is synced, clean, and free of pops/gaps
- [ ] All text is spelled correctly and matches approved info
- [ ] No temp music/SFX or placeholder graphics left in
- [ ] Color and exposure are consistent enough for lock
- [ ] Sequence/version name is correct
- [ ] Full watchthrough completed before lock

If you want, I can also turn this into:
1. a **one-page client-friendly checklist**,  
2. a **Google Docs / Notion format**, or  
3. a **more agency-style version** with tighter language.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and practical: it says it is a "practical, pre-picture-lock" checklist and even offers a "Shorter 'Must-Catch' Version" to avoid overkill.
Direct and plain throughout, e.g. "No temp shots, placeholders, or offline clips" and "Audio is synced, clean, and free of pops/gaps"—clear, no hedging.
Strong structure: organized into numbered sections with checkboxes, plus a separate shorter version and optional formats at the end.
Includes the main points plus key caveats, such as "unless approved," "obvious mismatched," and "if required," which keeps it focused without being bare-bones.
Uses direct, constructive checklist language that points out what can go wrong and how to fix it, like "Check for stray frames" and "Verify burn-ins, captions, or timecode if required."

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more picture-lock-specific version that stays short but catches the usual drift points:

## Pre–Picture Lock Checklist
**Editor:**  
**Project:**  
**Approved sequence/version:**  
**Date:**

- [ ] Open the exact approved sequence/version
- [ ] Confirm no older cuts, temp versions, or wrong exports are being used
- [ ] Check all edits for clean handles and no missing frames
- [ ] Review split edits / J-cuts / L-cuts for anything that needs a second look
- [ ] Make sure captions/subs are correct if they’re part of delivery
- [ ] Verify all on-screen text, names, and legal lines match the latest client round
- [ ] Check for leftover placeholders, temp graphics, or temp audio
- [ ] Spot-check audio sync, gaps, pops, and obvious level issues
- [ ] Confirm export notes/specs match the latest client feedback
- [ ] Do a full watchthrough before calling lock

## Short “must-catch” version
- [ ] Exact approved sequence/version is open
- [ ] Handles are clean; no missing frames
- [ ] Split edits/J-L cuts checked
- [ ] Captions/subs verified if needed
- [ ] Text/legal matches latest client round
- [ ] Export notes/specs match latest feedback
- [ ] Full watchthrough completed

If you want, I can also make this into a **super clean checkbox list for Google Docs** or a **slightly more polished client-facing version**.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and efficient: “here’s a tighter… version that stays short” and the checklist is compact, with a shorter duplicate version at the end.
Direct and plain throughout: “Open the exact approved sequence/version,” “Check for leftover placeholders,” “Do a full watchthrough before calling lock.” No hedging or vague language.
Well organized and step-by-step, using clear headings (“Pre–Picture Lock Checklist,” “Short ‘must-catch’ version”) and bullet-point workflow.
Gives the main points plus key caveats without overexplaining: includes important checks like “clean handles,” “missing frames,” “captions/subs,” and “export notes/specs,” but avoids theory.

Constructive and practical, pointing out what to check and how to fix risk areas (“Confirm no older cuts…,” “Verify… match the latest client round”). It’s helpful critique, though not explicitly corrective feedback on a draft.
